SGM (Small Graph Model) is the trained model, eventually a Graph-to-Graph function; the LLM (Qwen) is a translator layer at the language boundary, supplying language but never facts or logic; the simulator is the program that owns ground truth, dynamics, and scores.

The SGM will process language inside graph nodes, structure across edges, and multiple graphs plus non-graph data to produce graph output; until the deferred graph stage it reads and writes plain text. The LLM has three translator jobs: rendering simulator state into reports (outward), forgivingly interpreting SGM output into simulator actions (inward), and scoring language quality. The simulator computes per-step and per-game scores; nothing stores gold actions.

The notation replaced an earlier four-role draft that included a frontier-model role authoring an executable decision doctrine as an answer key; that draft encoded an imitation picture of stage-2 training and was reverted. In the settled design the LLM's inward job is what makes outcome training viable from the start: a forgiving interpreter maps near-miss outputs to the nearest sensible action, so a weakly trained model still receives nonzero outcome signal, the same move as scoring evaluation answers found anywhere in the output rather than requiring format. The listener's tolerance is annealed toward strictness and eventual removal, with correction distance logged as the progress metric toward canonical output.
